course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Rank, Consistency, Null Space, and Solution Sets
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, board-friendly, and quick to remember. Avoid abstract language unless the problem needs it.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to use pivots to find rank and nullity, decide whether Ax=b is consistent, and write solution sets using a particular solution plus the null space.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start by writing: rank = number of pivots; nullity = number of free variables.
Tell students that the null space solves Ax=0, so it is the movement that keeps b unchanged.
Do Problem 1 to connect RREF with rank and null space.
Do Problem 2 to practice consistency with ranks and an augmented matrix.
Do Problem 3 to explain the idea: all solutions = one solution + null space.
sayThisFirst: The null space is all vectors x where Ax=0.
If Ax=b has one solution p, then every other solution looks like p plus something from the null space.
Consistency just means at least one solution exists.
boardPlan: Write the rank-nullity idea at the top of the board.
Show that pivots control rank and free variables control nullity.
Use Problem 1 to read rank and null space from RREF.
Use Problem 2 to compare rank(A) and rank([A|b]).
Use Problem 3 to explain the geometry of p + Nul(A).
End by pointing students to Practice Lab Sections 2 and 3 for more rank/null space/subspace review.
commonMistakes: Using the number of rows instead of the number of pivot columns for rank.
Forgetting that nullity counts free variables, not zero rows.
Confusing the null space of A with the solution set of Ax=b.
Saying a system is inconsistent just because it has a free variable. Free variables usually mean infinitely many solutions, not no solution.
Forgetting that a particular solution must be included when solving Ax=b. The null space alone solves Ax=0.
notationWarnings: Write \(\operatorname{Nul}(A)\) or \(N(A)\) for the null space. Be consistent on the board.
Use \([A\mid b]\) for the augmented matrix, not just \(A\), when checking consistency of \(Ax=b\).
Do not write the null space as just one vector unless it is one-dimensional. Use \(\operatorname{span}\{\cdots\}\).
For nonhomogeneous systems, write \(x=p+t v\) or \(x=p+s v_1+t v_2\), not just \(t v\).

problems.title: Rank, nullity, and null space from RREF
problems.studentPromptLatex:
Suppose a \(3\times 4\) matrix \(A\) row-reduces to
\[
\operatorname{rref}(A)=\begin{bmatrix}
1&0&-2&0\
0&1&3&0\
0&0&0&1
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
Find \(\operatorname{rank}(A)\), the nullity of \(A\), and a basis for \(\operatorname{Nul}(A)\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: This is a reading problem. The RREF tells us where the pivots are. The null space comes from solving Ax=0, so there is no augmented column here.

visualSpec.purpose: Show pivot columns, free columns, rank, nullity, and the null-space direction.
boardSketchInstructions: Circle the pivots in columns 1, 2, and 4.
Mark column 3 as free and write \(x_3=t\).
Write the null-space vector vertically so students see the order \((x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)\).
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The RREF is exact. Rank is 3, nullity is 1, and the null space is exactly span{(2,-3,1,0)}.

walkthrough.label: Find the pivot columns
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Rank is just the pivot count. Here I see three pivot columns.

walkthrough.label: Find rank and nullity
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: One free variable means the null space will have one direction vector.

walkthrough.label: Solve the homogeneous system
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: For the null space, the right side is zero. Do not add an augmented column.

walkthrough.label: Use a parameter
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The free variable controls all the pivot variables.

walkthrough.label: Write the null space as a span
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The basis is the vector multiplying the parameter.

commonMistakes: Counting the zero row instead of counting pivot columns.
Forgetting that \(x_4\) is a pivot variable even though it appears by itself.
Putting the free variable in the wrong coordinate position.
Writing \((2,-3,1)\) instead of \((2,-3,1,0)\). The matrix has four columns, so vectors in the null space have four entries.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Sections 2 and 3: RREF, rank, null space, and subspace problems.

problems.title: Consistency from an augmented matrix
problems.studentPromptLatex:
A system in variables \(x,y,z\) row-reduces to
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1&0&2&\mid&4\
0&1&-1&\mid&3\
0&0&0&\mid&h-5
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
For which values of \(h\) is the system consistent? When it is consistent, write the solution set.
problems.whatToSayBefore: This problem is about the last row. The whole question is whether the last row becomes \(0=0\) or \(0=\text{nonzero}\).

visualSpec.purpose: Highlight the inconsistent row and compare rank(A) to rank([A|b]).
boardSketchInstructions: Set \(h-5=0\) to find the consistent case.
For \(h=5\), mark \(z\) as free and write \(z=t\).

If \(h
e5\), write rank(A)=2 but rank([A|b])=3, so no solution.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The system is consistent exactly when h=5. For h=5, the solution set is (4-2t, 3+t, t).

walkthrough.label: Read the last row
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Everything depends on whether the augmented entry is zero.

walkthrough.label: Find the consistent value
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: If h is 5, the last row is just 0=0, so it is harmless.

walkthrough.label: Explain the inconsistent case
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: A nonzero number in the augmented column with all zeros on the left means no solution.

walkthrough.label: Solve when h=5
walkthrough.latex:
\text{When }h=5,\text{ the matrix becomes}
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1&0&2&\mid&4\
0&1&-1&\mid&3\
0&0&0&\mid&0
\end{bmatrix}.
\]
\text{The variable }z\text{ is free, so let }z=t.
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Now there are two pivots and one free variable, so infinitely many solutions.

walkthrough.label: Translate the rows
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Move the z terms to the other side because z is the parameter.

walkthrough.label: Write the final solution set
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This is a line of solutions: one particular solution plus one null-space direction.

commonMistakes: Saying \(z=0\) just because there is no pivot in the z-column. Actually \(z\) is free.
Forgetting to check the last row before solving.
Writing one solution when the system has a free variable.

Mixing up the consistent case \(h=5\) with the inconsistent case \(h
e5\).
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 2: consistency, free variables, and parametric solution sets.

problems.title: All solutions are one solution plus the null space
problems.studentPromptLatex:
For a matrix \(A\), suppose one solution to \(Ax=b\) is
\[
p=\begin{bmatrix}1\0\2\-1\end{bmatrix}
\]
and
\[
\operatorname{Nul}(A)=\operatorname{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}1\-2\0\1\end{bmatrix},\begin{bmatrix}0\3\1\-1\end{bmatrix}\right\}.
\]
(a) Write the full solution set to \(Ax=b\).

(b) Decide whether
\[
q=\begin{bmatrix}3\-4\2\1\end{bmatrix}
\]
is also a solution.

(c) Decide whether
\[
r=\begin{bmatrix}1\3\4\-3\end{bmatrix}
\]
is also a solution.
problems.whatToSayBefore: This is the big idea problem. If p solves Ax=b, then adding anything from the null space still solves Ax=b. To check another vector, subtract p and see if the difference is in the null space.

visualSpec.purpose: Show the solution set as a shifted copy of the null space directions.
boardSketchInstructions: Draw a plane or sheet through \(p\), not through the origin, to represent \(p+\operatorname{Nul}(A)\).
Draw two direction arrows on the sheet for the two null-space basis vectors.
Say: the null space gives directions you can move without changing \(Ax\).
To test \(q\) and \(r\), subtract \(p\) and check if the difference is in the span of the null-space vectors.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: q is a solution because q-p = 2(1,-2,0,1). r is not a solution because r-p is not in the given null space.

walkthrough.label: Write the solution-set template
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The particular solution gets us to the right b. The null space lets us move around without changing b.

walkthrough.label: Plug in the particular solution and null-space basis
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Because there are two null-space basis vectors, I need two parameters.

walkthrough.label: Check q by subtracting p
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: We do not plug q into A because A is not given. Instead, use the null-space information.

walkthrough.label: See whether q-p is in the null space
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Since q differs from p by a null-space vector, q is also a solution.

walkthrough.label: Check r by subtracting p
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Now we do the same test for r.

walkthrough.label: Try to write r-p using the null-space basis
walkthrough.latex:
s\begin{bmatrix}1\-2\0\1\end{bmatrix}+t\begin{bmatrix}0\3\1\-1\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}s\-2s+3t\t\s-t\end{bmatrix}.

\text{To match }\begin{bmatrix}0\3\2\-2\end{bmatrix},\text{ the first entry gives }s=0\text{ and the third entry gives }t=2.

\text{Then the second entry would be }-2(0)+3(2)=6,\text{ not }3.
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The entries contradict each other, so r-p is not in the null space.

problems.finalAnswerLatex:
\text{All solutions: }\quad x=\begin{bmatrix}1\0\2\-1\end{bmatrix}+s\begin{bmatrix}1\-2\0\1\end{bmatrix}+t\begin{bmatrix}0\3\1\-1\end{bmatrix},\quad s,t\in\mathbb{R}.

q\text{ is a solution because }q-p=2\begin{bmatrix}1\-2\0\1\end{bmatrix}.

r\text{ is not a solution because }r-p
otin\operatorname{Nul}(A).
commonMistakes: Forgetting the particular solution and writing only the null space.
Thinking every vector in the null space is itself a solution to \(Ax=b\). It solves \(Ax=0\), not necessarily \(Ax=b\).
Trying to check \(q\) by multiplying by \(A\) even though \(A\) is not given.
Using only one parameter even though the null space has two basis vectors.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Sections 2 and 3: null space, subspaces, and solution-set geometry.

wrapUpQuestions: How do you find rank from RREF?
What row tells you that a system is inconsistent?
Why does \(p+\operatorname{Nul}(A)\) describe all solutions to \(Ax=b\)?
What is the difference between solving \(Ax=0\) and \(Ax=b\)?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 2
linksToWebsite.reason: More RREF, consistency, and solution-set practice.

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 3
linksToWebsite.reason: More span, subspace, basis, and null-space practice.

qualityControlNotes: All vectors in Problem 1 have four entries because A has four columns.
Problem 1 rank-nullity check: rank 3 + nullity 1 = 4 columns.
Problem 2 consistent exactly when h=5 because the final row becomes 0=0.
Problem 2 solution check: x=4-2t, y=3+t, z=t satisfies x+2z=4 and y-z=3.
Problem 3 q check: q-p=(2,-4,0,2)=2(1,-2,0,1), so q is a solution.
Problem 3 r check: r-p=(0,3,2,-2) cannot be s(1,-2,0,1)+t(0,3,1,-1) because s=0 and t=2 force second entry 6, not 3.
Notation intentionally uses \(\operatorname{Nul}(A)\), \([A\mid b]\), and vector-column formatting to reduce ambiguity.
reviewSummary: Session 04 is ready for Claude implementation. It emphasizes rank/nullity, consistency, and solution-set geometry with exact arithmetic and structured visual specifications.
